# ::marker styling

<FeatureLinks mdn="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/search?q=%3A%3Amarker%20styling%20CSS" caniuse="https://caniuse.com/?search=li" status="Pseudo 4 / pre-CR exception" label="Reference links" />

## Plain-language explanation

Control list marker content and styling.

## Representative syntax

```css
li::marker { color: red; content: counters(item, '.') '. '; }
```

## Demo

<CssDemo title="::marker styling" css-b64="" code-b64="" html-b64="" caption="The demo shows numbering and markers staying tied to document structure instead of being typed by hand." lang="en-US" badge="Practical demo" />

## When to use it

<div class="usage-grid">
<section>
  <h3>Best fit</h3>
  <p>Use ::marker styling in theme and state systems when you need to build color variations from semantic inputs instead of hand-tuning every state. It usually gives the stylesheet a clearer job than extra classes, wrappers, or script.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Example</h3>
  <p>A warning banner can derive its background, border, and text color from one token and still adapt in dark mode.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Watch out</h3>
  <p>Perceptual spaces help with ramps, but they do not replace contrast checks against real text sizes and backgrounds.</p>
</section>
</div>

## Source metadata

- Category: Generated Content, Lists, Counters, and Content References
- Maturity / source: Pseudo 4 / pre-CR exception
- Representative syntax: `li::marker { color: red; content: counters(item, '.') '. '; }`
- Position in category: 3 / 9
